topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification flashed on my phone screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. It was a direct message from Mr. Harrison. My breath hitched in my throat, the lukewarm tea in my hands suddenly feeling like ash. I stared at the screen, my fingers trembling so violently I almost dropped the device. He had tagged me in a photo from the school event—a picture I hadn't even realized he had taken. Every muscle in my body tightened; a cold, heavy pressure settled behind my ribs. I quickly minimized the app, my heart hammering against my sternum like a trapped bird. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to bolt from the room, to disappear into the shadows. How could he know? The thought spiraled, twisting into a knot of profound unease. I started pacing the small room, the floorboards groaning beneath my hurried steps, unable to settle.

[story 2]

I scrolled through my feed, a frantic, repetitive motion, searching for any sign that this was a glitch, a misunderstanding. Then I saw it: a post from Mr. Harrison’s account, a candid shot taken near the library entrance, with my face clearly visible in the background. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim. I gripped the edge of my desk until my knuckles were white, the cheap laminate digging painfully into my skin. I wanted to scream, to confront him, but the words caught in my throat, turning to a dry, useless rasp. I began scrubbing at my eyes, a futile attempt to erase the image from my mind, but the pixels remained stubbornly fixed. Every shadow in the room seemed to deepen, pressing in on me. I just needed this to stop.

[story 3]

It was late, the campus deserted save for the distant sound of the night security patrol. I was reviewing old notes when the phone buzzed. It was a reply from Mr. Harrison, a simple emoji, a single, cryptic smiley face. My hands began to shake uncontrollably. I dropped the phone onto the carpet, the sound surprisingly loud in the silence. I stood there, frozen, listening to the frantic thudding of my own pulse. The air felt thick, suffocating, as if the very oxygen was too much to draw in. I walked to the window, peering out at the dark quad, feeling utterly exposed, stripped bare under the silent scrutiny of the night. The need to flee the confines of my small apartment became an urgent, physical ache.
